subject: ap-psychology
title: Learning and Cognition — Token Economy, Reinforcement, and Memory
stimulus: Tomás is a 45-year-old high school history teacher. He is known for his fair grading and his ability to keep the class on task. One semester, he introduces a policy in which students earn points toward a class prize for completing optional reading assignments. Tomás has also noticed that over the years, the hallway outside his classroom has become increasingly noisy, and he finds it harder to concentrate on grading papers when students are changing classes. He is currently working with a therapist who is helping him recognize automatic negative thoughts about his abilities as a teacher and replace them with more balanced thinking.

parts.prompt: Explain how the token economy relates to Tomás's point system for optional reading assignments.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that a token economy is a behavior modification system in which individuals earn tokens (points) for desired behaviors that can later be exchanged for rewards, and that Tomás's system of awarding points for completing optional reading — redeemable for a class prize — follows this structure.

required_elements: Tomás's point system is a token economy: students earn points (tokens) for desired behavior (completing readings) which they can exchange for a reward (the class prize).
alternatives.correct_example: Tomás's point system is a token economy: students earn points (tokens) for desired behavior (completing readings) which they can exchange for a reward (the class prize).

required_elements: A token economy involves reinforcing target behaviors with tokens that are later exchanged for backup reinforcers; Tomás awards points for reading that students can exchange for a prize.
alternatives.correct_example: A token economy involves reinforcing target behaviors with tokens that are later exchanged for backup reinforcers; Tomás awards points for reading that students can exchange for a prize.

parts.prompt: Explain how the concept of negative reinforcement could relate to Tomás's grading behavior when the hallway is noisy.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that negative reinforcement involves removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase the likelihood of a behavior, and that Tomás might be more likely to grade papers during quiet times (e.g., during prep periods or after school) to escape or avoid the unpleasant noise — the removal of noise (aversive stimulus) reinforces his choice to grade during quiet periods.

required_elements: If Tomás moves his grading to quieter times to avoid the hallway noise, the reduction of noise (aversive stimulus) negatively reinforces the behavior of grading during those quieter times.
alternatives.correct_example: If Tomás moves his grading to quieter times to avoid the hallway noise, the reduction of noise (aversive stimulus) negatively reinforces the behavior of grading during those quieter times.

required_elements: Negative reinforcement explains how Tomás's avoidance of grading during noisy periods is strengthened by the removal of the unpleasant noise when he switches to grading at quieter times.
alternatives.correct_example: Negative reinforcement explains how Tomás's avoidance of grading during noisy periods is strengthened by the removal of the unpleasant noise when he switches to grading at quieter times.

parts.prompt: Explain how Weber's law could relate to Tomás's difficulty concentrating as the hallway noise level gradually increased over the years.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Weber's law states that the just noticeable difference (JND) between two stimuli is a constant proportion of the original stimulus, and that because the noise increased gradually over time, the incremental changes were below Tomás's just noticeable difference threshold, meaning he may not have perceived the gradual increase until the total noise level became significantly higher.

required_elements: Weber's law explains why Tomás may not have noticed the noise gradually becoming louder — each small increment in noise level fell below his JND, so he didn't perceive the incremental changes even as the total noise increased substantially.
alternatives.correct_example: Weber's law explains why Tomás may not have noticed the noise gradually becoming louder — each small increment in noise level fell below his JND, so he didn't perceive the incremental changes even as the total noise increased substantially.

required_elements: According to Weber's law, the more intense a background noise, the larger the increase needed for Tomás to notice a difference, meaning the gradual noise increase went undetected until it became quite disruptive.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Weber's law, the more intense a background noise, the larger the increase needed for Tomás to notice a difference, meaning the gradual noise increase went undetected until it became quite disruptive.

parts.prompt: Explain how the working memory model could relate to Tomás's difficulty grading during the noisy class change periods.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that working memory has a limited capacity for holding and manipulating information, and that the hallway noise places demands on the phonological loop (verbal/auditory component of working memory), competing with the cognitive processing required for grading and reducing Tomás's ability to concentrate.

required_elements: The hallway noise interferes with Tomás's working memory by overloading the phonological loop, which reduces the cognitive resources available for reading and evaluating student work.
alternatives.correct_example: The hallway noise interferes with Tomás's working memory by overloading the phonological loop, which reduces the cognitive resources available for reading and evaluating student work.

required_elements: Working memory has limited capacity; the noise during class changes competes for space in Tomás's working memory, making it harder for him to process and evaluate the information in the papers he is grading.
alternatives.correct_example: Working memory has limited capacity; the noise during class changes competes for space in Tomás's working memory, making it harder for him to process and evaluate the information in the papers he is grading.

parts.prompt: Explain how cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) could relate to how Tomás's therapist is helping him.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that CBT targets automatic negative thoughts by helping clients identify, evaluate, and replace distorted cognitions with more realistic thinking, which is what Tomás's therapist is doing when helping him recognize and restructure his negative thoughts about his teaching abilities.

required_elements: CBT is directly applicable: Tomás's therapist is using the cognitive component of CBT to help him challenge and replace automatic negative thoughts about himself as a teacher with more balanced, accurate thinking.
alternatives.correct_example: CBT is directly applicable: Tomás's therapist is using the cognitive component of CBT to help him challenge and replace automatic negative thoughts about himself as a teacher with more balanced, accurate thinking.

required_elements: Through CBT, Tomás learns to identify the negative automatic thoughts (e.g., 'I am a poor teacher') and evaluate evidence for and against them, replacing them with more rational beliefs.
alternatives.correct_example: Through CBT, Tomás learns to identify the negative automatic thoughts (e.g., 'I am a poor teacher') and evaluate evidence for and against them, replacing them with more rational beliefs.

parts.prompt: Explain how the overjustification effect could relate to how Tomás's point system might affect students' intrinsic motivation for reading.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the overjustification effect occurs when an external reward causes a decrease in a person's intrinsic motivation for an activity they previously found internally rewarding, and that Tomás's point system for optional reading might reduce students' natural love of reading because they begin to attribute their reading behavior to earning points rather than to genuine interest.

required_elements: The overjustification effect predicts that once the point system is in place, students who previously enjoyed reading for its own sake may come to see reading as something done for external rewards (points), reducing their intrinsic motivation.
alternatives.correct_example: The overjustification effect predicts that once the point system is in place, students who previously enjoyed reading for its own sake may come to see reading as something done for external rewards (points), reducing their intrinsic motivation.

required_elements: If students who already loved reading begin earning points for it, the overjustification effect suggests their internal motivation may decrease — they will attribute their reading to the reward, not personal interest.
alternatives.correct_example: If students who already loved reading begin earning points for it, the overjustification effect suggests their internal motivation may decrease — they will attribute their reading to the reward, not personal interest.

parts.prompt: Explain how the bystander effect could relate to the behavior of students in the noisy hallway outside Tomás's classroom.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the bystander effect occurs when the presence of others decreases the likelihood that any one individual will intervene in a situation, and that students in the noisy hallway may each individually fail to quiet down because each student assumes someone else is responsible for reducing the noise or that the collective noise is acceptable because everyone is participating.

required_elements: The bystander effect predicts that individual students are less likely to moderate their own noise because they look to others in the crowd and diffuse responsibility — each student assumes others will lower the noise level.
alternatives.correct_example: The bystander effect predicts that individual students are less likely to moderate their own noise because they look to others in the crowd and diffuse responsibility — each student assumes others will lower the noise level.

required_elements: In the crowded hallway, each student is less likely to reduce their own noise level because the presence of many others leads to diffusion of responsibility, a feature of the bystander effect.
alternatives.correct_example: In the crowded hallway, each student is less likely to reduce their own noise level because the presence of many others leads to diffusion of responsibility, a feature of the bystander effect.
